stem: A computer's primary storage includes both RAM and ROM.
model_answer: RAM is volatile, meaning its contents are lost when the power is removed. ROM is non-volatile, meaning its contents are kept even with the power off. RAM is used to hold programs and data the CPU is currently working on; ROM holds the bootloader / firmware that the computer needs to start up.
review_notes: Teacher-marked. Look for the volatile/non-volatile contrast and an example of why each is suited to its job. 'Permanent' vs 'temporary' is acceptable wording but the term 'volatile' should be recognised.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Explain what is meant by volatile and non-volatile storage, using RAM and ROM as examples.

mark_points.text: Volatile = loses contents when the power is removed (RAM is volatile)
Non-volatile = keeps contents when the power is removed (ROM is non-volatile)
